title: Iterators in Rust
content:
### Iterators in Rust

Iterators are a core abstraction in Rust for processing sequences of values. They are lazy by default and drive a rich set of combinator methods.

---

### Iterator Traits

| Trait | Method | Description |
|-------|--------|-------------|
| `Iterator` | `next()` | Returns `Option<Item>` |
| `IntoIterator` | `into_iter()` | Converts into iterator |

---

### Common Iterator Methods

| Method | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| `map(|x| ...)` | Transform each element |
| `filter(|x| ...)` | Keep elements matching predicate |
| `fold(init, |acc, x| ...)` | Accumulate values |
| `collect()` | Collect into collection |
| `take(n)` | Take first n elements |
| `skip(n)` | Skip first n elements |
| `enumerate()` | Add index to elements |
| `zip(other)` | Pair with another iterator |
| `any(|x| ...)` | Check if any match |
| `all(|x| ...)` | Check if all match |

---

### Examples

```rust
let numbers = vec![1, 2, 3, 4, 5];

// Lazy iteration - nothing executes until collected
let doubled: Vec<i32> = numbers.iter()
    .map(|x| x * 2)
    .collect();

// Chaining
let result: i32 = numbers.iter()
    .filter(|x| *x % 2 == 0)
    .map(|x| x * x)
    .sum();

// Infinite iterators
let first_ten: Vec<i32> = (0..).take(10).collect();
```

> **Key Point:** Iterators in Rust are lazy and zero-cost. The compiler optimizes iterator chains into efficient loops with no overhead. Prefer iterator methods over manual loops for clarity and performance.

quiz.question: Are Rust iterators eager or lazy?
